did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning techniques, specifically a custom-built Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a modified InceptionV3 model, to perform binary classification of skin cancer (malignant melanoma vs. benign nevus) using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of two deep learning models (a custom CNN and a modified InceptionV3) for skin cancer detection using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology, training parameters, and quantitative results (e.g., 95.72% accuracy for InceptionV3), which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: A robust CNN Deep Learning and InceptionV3 model Techniques for Enhanced Skin Cancer Detection
journal: Journal of Al-Qadisiyah for Computer Science and Mathematics
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin cancer detection by developing and evaluating two deep learning models: a custom-built Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a modified InceptionV3 model. The research utilized the HAM10000 dataset to perform binary classification, distinguishing between malignant melanoma and benign nevus lesions. The models were evaluated using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC. The custom CNN achieved an accuracy of 91.80%, while the modified InceptionV3 model secured a higher accuracy of 95.72%. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of both tailored and pre-trained deep learning architectures in enhancing diagnostic accuracy for skin cancer.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes used in the binary classification task. The HAM10000 dataset contains the abbreviation 'mel' for this class.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin cancer lesions into malignant melanoma and benign nevus categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the primary objective is to perform binary classification, distinguishing between malignant melanoma and benign nevus lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The models (Custom CNN and Modified InceptionV3) use softmax activation for the final layer to forecast image classes, and performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine 10000)
datasets_used.description: A widely acknowledged benchmark dataset for skin lesion classification sourced from the ISIC repository. It comprises 10,015 dermatoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions. The study utilized a subset of this dataset for binary classification.

use.training: Used as the primary dataset for training the Custom CNN and Modified InceptionV3 models. The study notes that the dataset exhibits substantial class imbalance with almost two-thirds of photos categorized under the 'nv' class.
use.validation: Used for validation with a typical split of 0.2 (20%) of the training data.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation. The models were executed on the training subset and assessed on the testing subset of the HAM10000 dataset.
use.other: The InceptionV3 model was pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset before being fine-tuned on HAM10000.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: A large-scale image database used for pre-training the InceptionV3 architecture.

use.training: Used to provide pre-trained weights for the InceptionV3 model base architecture.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.

HAM10000.notes: The models were evaluated on the testing subset of the HAM10000 dataset. The Modified InceptionV3 model outperformed the Custom CNN across all reported metrics. The study also compared these results with previous works using the same dataset, noting that previous methods achieved accuracies ranging from 74.75% to 82.95%.

architecture_used.main_model: Two models: Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Modified InceptionV3
architecture_used.pretraining: Modified InceptionV3 was pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset. Custom CNN details not specified.

layers: Convolutional layers with 3x3 filters (sizes: 32, 64, 128) and 'same' padding

modifications: Removed highest level of classification layer
Global Average Pooling 2D layer applied to base output
Dense layer with 1024 units and ReLU activation
Final dense layer with softmax activation

classification_or_output_head.custom_cnn: Softmax layer for class probabilities followed by a classification layer
classification_or_output_head.modified_inceptionv3: Final dense layer with softmax activation for multi-class classification

preprocessing_techniques: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for contrast enhancement
Gaussian Blur filter with a 5x5 pixel kernel for noise reduction
Edge sharpening using a 3x3 kernel to enhance high-frequency edge elements

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used as part of the data augmentation generator to enlarge images progressively during training.
Used as part of the data augmentation generator to alter the orientation of images during training.
Used as part of the data augmentation generator to mirror images during training.
Used as part of the data augmentation generator to magnify portions of images during training.
Used as part of the data augmentation generator to introduce random noise to images during training.

conclusions: The modified InceptionV3 model achieved a higher accuracy of 95.72% compared to the custom CNN's 91.80% accuracy in binary classification of malignant melanoma and benign nevus using the HAM10000 dataset.
Both the custom-built Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and the modified InceptionV3 model demonstrated effectiveness in enhancing diagnostic accuracy and efficiency for skin cancer detection.
Deep learning techniques show significant potential to improve skin cancer diagnosis accuracy, reduce diagnostic delays, and lead to better patient outcomes.
The study highlights the capability of tailored and pre-trained deep learning models to enhance diagnostic precision even when encountering various resource and computing constraints.
The modified InceptionV3 model, pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned on HAM10000, outperformed the custom CNN architecture in terms of overall performance metrics.
important_remarks: The study is limited to binary classification (malignant melanoma vs. benign nevus), ignoring other HAM10000 classes such as basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, and dermatofibroma, which limits generalizability to broader skin cancer detection tasks.

The HAM10000 dataset exhibits substantial class imbalance, with almost two-thirds of images categorized as benign nevus (nv), which may bias the model towards the majority class despite the reported high accuracy.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between the Custom CNN and Modified InceptionV3 models.

External validation is lacking; the models were only evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, and no independent external dataset was used to assess generalizability across different populations or imaging devices.

The comparison with previous works in Table 4 is methodologically weak, as it compares the current study's results against methods using different datasets (e.g., Dermques, ISBI 2012) or different classification tasks, making direct performance comparisons invalid.

The paper does not specify the exact train/validation/test split ratios or the number of samples per class used in the experiment, raising concerns about reproducibility and potential data leakage if the split was not strictly stratified.
The Modified InceptionV3 model was pre-trained on ImageNet (natural images) rather than dermatoscopic images, which may introduce domain shift issues not addressed in the analysis.
The study claims computational efficiency but does not report inference time, model size, or FLOPs, which are critical metrics for assessing deployment feasibility in clinical settings.
The preprocessing pipeline (CLAHE, Gaussian Blur, Edge Sharpening) is applied uniformly without ablation studies to determine the individual contribution of each step to the final performance.

The paper does not discuss potential biases in the HAM10000 dataset regarding patient demographics (e.g., skin type, age, gender) or geographic origin, which could affect model fairness and performance in diverse clinical populations.
